item.title: Halfway Through 2026: What the Summer Economy Is Really Telling Us
item.body:
As the second half of 2026 begins, Americans are getting a clearer read on an economy that spent the first six months defying easy labels. Growth has held up better than many forecasters feared a year ago, yet the recovery still feels uneven depending on where you sit — and late June offered several signals worth watching.

## The consumer is cautious, not exhausted

Summer is the great American spending season, and the data from Memorial Day through the end of June is one of the most reliable early tells of consumer confidence for the rest of the year. This year the pattern is nuanced: households are still traveling, dining out, and booking experiences, but they are trading down more deliberately — choosing the cheaper flight, the shorter trip, the store-brand cart. That "spending, but watching the receipt" behavior is the story of 2026 in miniature.

Key things to keep an eye on:

- **Services vs. goods.** Money is still flowing toward experiences (travel, live events, dining) faster than toward physical goods.
- **The low end feels the squeeze first.** Lower-income households have less cushion left from the pandemic-era savings buffer, and their pullback tends to show up earliest.
- **Big-ticket hesitation.** Cars, appliances, and home improvements remain sensitive to borrowing costs.

## The Fed's balancing act

The central question hanging over the summer is the path of interest rates. The Federal Reserve continues to weigh a labor market that has cooled from red-hot to merely warm against inflation that has proven stickier in some corners — housing and services in particular — than the headline numbers suggest. Every jobs report and inflation print between now and the fall meetings will be parsed for hints about the timing and size of any move.

For ordinary readers, the practical translation is simple: borrowing is still expensive relative to the 2010s, but the direction of travel matters more than the level. A credible path toward lower rates would ease mortgages, small-business loans, and credit-card burdens over time.

## Why the next 90 days matter

The back half of the year is when holiday inventory decisions, hiring plans, and corporate budgets get locked in. What businesses see in the summer shapes what they do in the fall. If consumer demand stays resilient through July and August, expect a steadier hiring picture into year-end. If it wobbles, caution spreads quickly.

**The bottom line:** 2026 so far is a story of an economy that is neither booming nor breaking — it is grinding forward, and the summer is the season that decides which way the momentum tips.
